title: Latency vs Accuracy Tradeoffs
concept: Quantifying how search depth increases latency, and finding the sweet spot for utility.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson you compared planners on the same puzzle. Which planner explores many branching ideas before picking one path?

options.text: A single guess with no steps

exercises.explain: Tree of Thoughts branches out into many possible thought paths and compares them, unlike ReAct which loops one action at a time. More branches can mean better answers but also more work.

cards.title: Fast or Perfect?
cards.body: Imagine an agent that thinks longer to give a better answer. But every extra second of thinking makes the user wait. When is 'good enough' actually the smartest choice?

cards.title: Latency vs Accuracy
cards.body: Latency is how long the agent takes to reply. Accuracy is how right the answer is. Searching deeper (more thinking steps) usually raises accuracy but also raises latency.

cards.title: The Pizza Delivery Rule
cards.body: A cook could triple-check every pizza for perfection, but customers hate waiting an hour. The best kitchens find the point where the pizza is great AND arrives fast.

cards.title: Depth Costs Time
cards.body: Searching 2 steps deep answers in 1 second at 80% accuracy. Going 5 steps deep hits 92% but takes 6 seconds. That extra 12% cost 5 whole seconds of waiting.

cards.title: Myth: Deeper Is Always Better
cards.body: Not true! After a point, extra search depth adds tiny accuracy gains for huge time costs. The smart move is to stop at the 'sweet spot' where utility peaks.

cards.title: Shallow vs Deep Search
cards.body: Shallow search is snappy but can miss the best answer. Deep search is thorough but slow. The sweet spot balances both.

exercises.prompt: An agent's accuracy jumps from 70% to 88% when going from depth 1 to depth 3, then only to 90% at depth 6 (which takes 4x longer). Where is the smart sweet spot?

options.text: Depth 3 — big accuracy gain for reasonable time
Depth 6 — always pick the highest accuracy

exercises.explain: Depth 3 gives most of the accuracy boost (70%→88%). Going to depth 6 costs 4x the time for only 2% more. The sweet spot is where you get big gains before the returns shrink.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Increasing search depth usually increases both accuracy and latency.
exercises.explain: Deeper search means exploring more possibilities, which tends to find better answers (higher accuracy) but takes more time (higher latency). Both go up together, so you must balance them.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about the balance point.
exercises.sentence: The sweet spot is where extra thinking stops being worth the added latency.
exercises.explain: Latency is the waiting time. Beyond the sweet spot, more search adds latency without meaningfully boosting accuracy, so it is no longer worth it.

exercises.prompt: Which answer was written by an AI trying to sound like a helpful tradeoff tip?

options.text: To optimize agent performance, one must judiciously balance search depth against response latency, halting exploration once marginal accuracy gains no longer justify the incremental time cost.
just make it think less if people are waiting too long lol
My agent takes forever now, how do I make it faster??

exercises.explain: Option A has the polished, formal phrasing ('judiciously balance', 'marginal accuracy gains') typical of AI text. The others sound like casual, real human chat.

exercises.prompt: Your planning agent hits 89% accuracy at depth 4 (2 seconds) and 91% at depth 8 (9 seconds). Users abandon the app if replies take over 3 seconds. What is the best choice, and why?

options.text: Use depth 4 — the 2% accuracy gain from depth 8 isn't worth losing users to slow replies
Use depth 8 — accuracy is the only thing that matters
Use depth 1 — speed is the only thing that matters
Randomly switch depths so no one notices

exercises.explain: Utility means accuracy AND user experience. Depth 8 breaks the 3-second limit for a tiny 2% gain, driving users away. Depth 4 keeps replies fast and accurate enough — that's the real sweet spot where total value peaks.
